To create documentation of a method in TypeScript that will provide mouse-over information in VS Code, you can use JSDoc comments. JSDoc comments are a standard way of documenting code in JavaScript and TypeScript, providing annotations about parameter types, return types, and additional context.
The format for JSDoc comments in TypeScript includes a description of the method, descriptions of each parameter with their types, and a description of the return value.
When hovering over the method name in VS Code, the JSDoc comments are displayed as a tooltip, showing the method's description, parameter details, and return type.

To document variables in an interface using JSDoc, add comments to each variable in the format `/** This is a description of variable */`. For example, in the interface `MyInterface`, each variable like `variable1`, `variable2`, and `variable3` can be annotated with a JSDoc comment.
When hovering over a variable name in a code editor, the JSDoc comment appears as a tooltip, providing a brief description of the variable.

Ernest Hemingway's "The Old Man and the Sea" uses the setting of the ocean to create a sense of isolation and solitude for the old fisherman, Santiago. The vast, open sea and its unpredictable nature contribute to the story's melancholic mood and the sense of existential struggle.
tags: The Old Man and the Sea

semantic_nodes.semantic_memory: F. Scott Fitzgerald's "The Great Gatsby" uses the setting of the glittering party scene in the roaring 1920s to create the giddy optimism of the Jazz Age. The lavish mansions, chic speakeasies, and frenzied dance parties contribute to the story's tone of optimism, excess, and doomed idealism.

"To Build a Fire" by Jack London is set in the harsh, unforgiving landscape of the Yukon during the Klondike Gold Rush. The freezing temperatures, biting winds, and icy terrain create an atmosphere of danger and imminent death. The protagonist, warned by an old-timer not to travel alone, disregards this advice and ventures out with only his dog, highlighting the man's arrogance and folly.

In Shirley Jackson's "The Lottery," the setting of a seemingly ordinary small town on a sunny day in late June contrasts sharply with the brutality and senseless violence of the annual lottery event, contributing to an unsettling mood.

"To Build a Fire" is a very intense and gripping story where the harsh conditions of the Yukon wilderness make the man's journey perilous, creating a sense of danger and urgency for both the character and the reader. The setting also emphasizes the man's isolation, intensifying his struggle against the elements. Overall, this setting contributes to the story's bleak and fatalistic tone.

Colson Whitehead's Pulitzer Prize-winning novel "The Underground Railroad" is set in the American South during the 1800s and follows the journey of a slave named Cora who escapes from a Georgia plantation and travels north via the Underground Railroad. The different settings, ranging from the brutal conditions of the plantation to the seemingly idyllic town of South Carolina, underscore the brutality and inhumanity of slavery and contribute to the sense of tension and suspense as Cora navigates the dangers of the underground railroad and evades slave-catchers.

Tommy Orange's novel "There There" is set in modern-day Oakland, California, and follows a group of Native American characters preparing for a powwow. The setting, blending urban and natural landscapes, serves as a metaphor for the struggles of Native Americans in contemporary society, contributing to themes of displacement, identity, and cultural heritage. The contrast between the urban landscape and traditional powwow grounds highlights the tension and conflict central to the exploration of the Native American experience in modern America.

"There There" is a powerful and thought-provoking novel that examines the complexities of Native American identity and experience in modern America. Its use of setting and landscape is particularly striking, creating a layered and nuanced portrayal of contemporary Native American life.
The changes in Hulme have led to gentrification, increasing property prices and rents, making the area less affordable for low-income families and forcing many to relocate to cheaper nearby suburbs.

The regeneration of Hulme has improved transportation infrastructure and access to public transport, including the extension of the Metrolink tram system to other areas and suburbs, enhancing connectivity and accessibility.

Regeneration projects can benefit other deprived areas in the city, but they should be approached carefully and with consideration for the needs and desires of the local community. They should balance regeneration with the preservation of the area's identity and culture.

Regeneration projects should be inclusive, affordable, and accessible to the existing community, rather than alienating them. They should take into account the wider social, economic, and environmental impacts, such as job creation, environmental sustainability, and reducing social inequalities.

Public meetings can be held where local residents, community groups, and businesses can voice their opinions and concerns about the proposed regeneration projects. These meetings can be conducted both in person and virtually.
Focus groups can be formed to discuss specific aspects of the regeneration project, allowing for targeted discussions on issues affecting different groups within the community.
Local community members can participate in design workshops where they collaborate with architects and urban designers to develop ideas and plans for the regeneration project.
Social media platforms and online surveys can be utilized to gather broader community feedback and opinions on the regeneration project.
Community-led initiatives can be established where local residents develop their own proposals and solutions for the regeneration project.

The Levenshulme Bee Network is a community-led project in Levenshulme, a suburb of Manchester, aimed at improving public transportation and active travel infrastructure. The project involves the creation of a network of cycling and walking routes that are safe, accessible, and environmentally friendly.

The Real Food Wythenshawe project is a community-led initiative in Wythenshawe, a suburban area in the south of Manchester, that aims to improve access to healthy and affordable food. The project includes the creation of community gardens, food co-operatives, and education programs.

The Hulme Community Garden Centre, located in the Hulme district of Manchester, is a social enterprise that offers gardening and horticultural training for local residents. The center sells plants, urban agriculture tools, and other garden-related items, with profits reinvested into the local community to provide employment opportunities and promote environmental sustainability.

PlayPlay is a video creation platform that enables businesses and individuals to easily create engaging videos without requiring technical skills or extensive video production experience. It simplifies the video creation process and provides a user-friendly interface to produce high-quality videos quickly.

PlayPlay offers a variety of templates, customization options, and access to a media library containing millions of images and videos, facilitating the creation of visually appealing videos that capture the audience's attention.

PlayPlay’s platform is optimized for social media platforms, enabling users to create videos tailored specifically for Instagram, Facebook, and Twitter. This feature helps users promote their products or services, showcase their brand, and connect with their audience in an engaging, informative, and entertaining manner.

Hawaii, USA, offers stunning beaches, lush scenery, and a range of outdoor activities. Families can enjoy snorkeling, surfing, and luaus, while solo travelers can explore hidden gems, hike, or simply relax on the beach.

Cancun, Mexico, offers an array of all-inclusive resorts, beautiful beaches, and a vibrant city center. Solo travelers can enjoy water sports, cultural attractions, and a lively nightlife, while families can appreciate the kid-friendly amenities and activities.

Bali, Indonesia, features stunning beaches, temples, and vibrant culture. Families can enjoy water sports, yoga, and wellness retreats, while solo travelers can explore the island’s spiritual side, hike, or take a surf lesson.

New York City, USA, has something for everyone. Solo travelers can explore iconic landmarks, museums, and Broadway shows, while families can enjoy kid-friendly attractions like the Statue of Liberty, Central Park, and the American Museum of Natural History.

Paris, France, is a romantic destination for solo travelers, with its art museums, historic landmarks, and charming cafes. Families can enjoy the Eiffel Tower, Disneyland Paris, and the Luxembourg Gardens.

Singapore offers a blend of Asian and Western cultures. Solo travelers can explore Gardens by the Bay, Marina Bay Sands, and the food scene, while families can enjoy Universal Studios Singapore, the Singapore Zoo, and Sentosa Island.

Yellowstone National Park, USA, is a nature lover's paradise, with geysers, hot springs, and an abundance of wildlife. Solo travelers can hike, camp, and explore, while families can enjoy guided tours, scenic drives, and outdoor activities like horseback riding.

Costa Rica offers lush rainforests, beautiful beaches, and eco-lodges. Solo travelers can surf, zip line, or hike, while families can enjoy wildlife watching, rafting, and relaxing in a beachside villa.

New Zealand offers a range of outdoor activities, from hiking and biking to bungee jumping and skydiving. Solo travelers can explore the scenic routes, national parks, and adventure sports, while families can enjoy the scenic beauty, wildlife encounters, and outdoor activities like kayaking and horseback riding.

Volcanoes National Park on the Big Island offers exploration of the Kilauea volcano, Thurston Lava Tube, and the Jaggar Museum. Visitors can take guided hikes or explore independently to see lava flows, steam vents, and scenic landscapes.

Mauna Kea, the tallest mountain in Hawaii, provides opportunities for stargazing at the Mauna Kea Visitor Information Station. Guided tours to the summit are available for sunrise or stargazing experiences.
Snorkeling and scuba diving can be enjoyed at locations such as Kealakekua Bay, Hapuna Beach, and Honokaope Bay, where visitors can discover the island’s vibrant marine life.
Ziplining adventures are offered by companies like Kohala Zipline or Zip Isle, allowing visitors to fly through the lush Hawaiian rainforest.
Horseback riding tours are available through outfitters such as Na'alapa Stables or Paniolo Horseback Adventures, providing a way to explore the island's countryside.
The Hilo Farmers Market, open on Saturdays, features local produce, artisanal goods, and a lively atmosphere.
The Pacific Tsunami Museum educates visitors about the island’s history with tsunamis and their impact on the community.
The Hawaii Tropical Botanical Garden showcases over 2,000 species of plants and flowers, offering a serene environment for strolling.
Kaloko-Honokohau National Historical Park includes ancient Hawaiian temples, fishponds, and petroglyphs, located along the coastline.

Kailua-Kona is a great choice for exploring the town, doing coffee farm tours, and enjoying a laid-back, local vibe. The area is known for its rich coffee culture, stunning natural beauty, and relaxed atmosphere.
Kona Tiki Hotel offers a retro-style, relaxed atmosphere with rooms starting at around $120 per night. It is located right on the waterfront.
Kona Inn provides a cozy, family-run hotel experience with a tropical vibe, offering rooms from $100 per night.
Kona Hostel caters to solo travelers with a sociable environment, providing dorms and private rooms starting at $30 per night for a dorm bed.
Holualoa Inn is a charming B&B with a lush garden, located in a historic building, and offers rooms starting at around $180 per night.
Kona Makai Condos offer comfortable, self-catering condos with a relaxed atmosphere, priced from $150 per night.
Wyndham Kona Hawaiian Resort provides a laid-back vibe with studios and one-bedroom condos starting at $180 per night.
Coffee Shack is a quirky, eco-friendly hostel with private rooms and dorms located on a coffee farm, starting at $40 per night.
Kona Coffee Living History Farm is a working coffee farm offering guided tours and tastings.

The Hilo Farmers Market is a highlight of the island where local farmers, artisans, and vendors sell fresh produce, flowers, crafts, and more every Saturday. Visitors can find unique souvenirs, taste delicious local specialties, and experience the island's vibrant culture.
Rainbow Falls, located just a few miles from downtown Hilo, is an 80-foot waterfall that visitors can hike to or view from the parking area.
Wailoa River State Recreation Area offers scenic drives, hikes, kayaking, and boat tours among lush tropical surroundings.
The Pacific Tsunami Museum provides information about the history of tsunamis and their impact on the island.
Hilo Town features charming shops, restaurants, cafes, and the Hilo Public Library, an example of Art Deco architecture.
Akaka Falls State Park, located about 15 minutes north of Hilo, includes stunning waterfalls, hiking trails, and lush tropical surroundings.

To maximize a day trip to Hilo from Kailua-Kona, it is recommended to leave early (around 7:00-8:00 am), spend a few hours at the Hilo Farmers Market and Rainbow Falls, and then visit either the Wailoa River State Recreation Area or Akaka Falls State Park in the afternoon before returning to Kailua-Kona in the late afternoon.
The drive from Kailua-Kona to Hilo takes approximately 1.5 hours, depending on traffic conditions.
Guided tours or shuttle services can be considered for those uncomfortable with driving between Kailua-Kona and Hilo.
Cafe Pesto is a Hilo institution known for serving delicious, farm-to-table cuisine with a focus on local ingredients. They are famous for their pesto burger and fresh fish dishes.
Hilo Bay Cafe offers waterfront dining with stunning views of Hilo Bay. Their menu features a mix of Hawaiian, Asian, and American flavors.
Kuhio Grille is a casual, family-owned eatery that serves classic Hawaiian comfort food, including laulau, kalua pig, and loco moco.
The Fish and The Hog is a popular spot for seafood lovers, offering fresh, sustainable seafood dishes with a focus on local flavors.
Hilo Town Tavern is a gastropub that serves creative, farm-to-table cuisine with a focus on local ingredients and craft beers.
Miyo's is a small, family-run restaurant serving delicious, authentic Japanese cuisine with a focus on local ingredients.
Hilo Farmers Market Food Stalls offer a variety of quick, delicious bites, ranging from traditional Hawaiian dishes to international cuisine.
Kalapana Cafe is a casual cafe that serves tasty, locally-inspired sandwiches, salads, and baked goods.
Punalu'u Bakery offers a variety of sweet and savory treats, including traditional Hawaiian breads and pastries.
Shave Ice Co. and Anuenue Ice Cream are local shops where visitors can try the classic Hawaiian treat, shave ice.
Helena's Hawaiian Food has been serving traditional Hawaiian dishes for over 70 years. Their laulau is made with tender pork and beef, wrapped in taro leaves and steamed to perfection.
Kuhio Grille offers a delicious laulau plate lunch, served with rice and macaroni salad.
Hilo Bay Cafe serves laulau made with fresh, locally-sourced ingredients and includes a side of poi made from taro root.
Cafe Pesto serves kalua pig that is slow-cooked in an underground oven called an imu, accompanied by cabbage, rice, and poi.
At the Hilo Farmers Market Food Stalls, you can find several food stalls offering kalua pig, typically served with cabbage and rice.
Miyo's, a small, family-run restaurant, offers a delicious kalua pig plate lunch, served with rice and macaroni salad.
Poi, a traditional Hawaiian staple made from fermented taro root, is commonly served as a side dish at many local eateries.
Lomi salmon, a classic Hawaiian dish made with salted salmon, diced tomatoes, onions, and chili peppers, is found on the menu at many local restaurants.
Haupia, a traditional Hawaiian coconut milk dessert similar to pudding, is available on the menu at many local eateries and bakeries.
User needs help finding a good shoe polish for their brown leather boots. They have a kit from Amazon but are unsure if it's suitable. The boots were worn on Thursday and are starting to look dull.

Agent suggests discussing the details of the Amazon kit, including the brand name and type of polish (wax-based, oil-based, cream-based). The agent also asks if the user has tried using it on the boots and the outcome.
For brown leather boots, choose a polish that matches the color of the boots, such as "dark brown" or "chestnut" for dark brown boots, and "medium brown" or "tan" for light brown boots.
Opt for a high-quality, wax-based polish containing natural waxes like beeswax or carnauba wax to provide shine and protection against water and scratches.
Consider a polish with conditioning properties containing ingredients like lanolin or coconut oil to moisturize and soften the leather.
Popular shoe polish brands recommended for brown leather boots include Kiwi, Allen Edmonds, Saphir, Lincoln, and Chamber's.
If the user is unsatisfied with the results from the Amazon kit, they can try a different polish.
Kiwi offers a Water Repellent Spray that can be used on leather, suede, and nubuck. It helps to repel water and stains and is safe to use on most types of leather.
Allen Edmonds offers a Water Repellent Spray specifically designed for leather shoes and boots. It's a silicone-based formula that helps to repel water and stains and is easy to apply.
Saphir offers a waterproofing spray called Saphir Rain & Snow for leather, suede, and nubuck. It helps to repel water and stains and is safe to use on most types of leather.
Chamber's offers a Water Repellent Spray for leather, suede, and nubuck. It helps to repel water and stains and is safe to use on most types of leather.
Waterproofing creams and waxes can provide an extra layer of protection against water and stains and can help to condition the leather.
Nikwax Tech Wax is a waterproofing wax designed for leather, suede, and nubuck. It's suitable for boots exposed to heavy rain or snow.
Sno-Seal Waterproofing Cream is designed for leather, suede, and nubuck. It's ideal for boots regularly exposed to water and stains.

Finding a reliable and skilled cobbler can be challenging, but asking friends, family, or coworkers for recommendations can provide valuable insights into the quality of work and customer service.

Checking online review platforms like Yelp, Google, or Facebook Reviews can help you see what other customers have to say about local shoe repair services, especially those that mention similar repairs like heel repair.
Online directories such as the Shoe Service Institute of America (SSIA) or the National Shoe Repair Association (NSRA) can be used to find certified cobblers in your area.
Checking the social media presence of local shoe repair services on platforms like Facebook, Instagram, or Twitter can help you see their work, read reviews, and assess their expertise.
A professional website can indicate a well-established business and should list services, prices, and testimonials.
Visiting the cobbler's shop in person allows you to look for a clean, organized workspace and request to see examples of their work to gauge craftsmanship and attention to detail.

City Winery, situated at 11 W. Riverwalk South, is a unique spot combining a restaurant, wine bar, and winery. It offers globally inspired small plates and hosts live music performances.
Chicago Brewhouse, located at 233 N. Canal St, serves classic American fare such as burgers, sandwiches, and salads. It provides river views from its outdoor patio and has a lively indoor atmosphere.
Harry Caray's Italian Steakhouse, at 33 W. Kinzie St, is known for its signature dishes including steaks, pasta, and seafood. It offers an upscale dining experience near the Riverwalk.
The Berghoff, located at 17 W. Adams St, is a historic German restaurant established in 1887. It is famous for its root beer, sausages, and schnitzel in a cozy, old-world setting.
L2O, located at 2300 N. Lincoln Park W, is about a 10-minute walk from the Riverwalk and offers an upscale dining experience focusing on fresh fish and seafood dishes.

Intelligentsia Coffee is a Chicago staple for coffee and pastries, with multiple locations near the Riverwalk, including 53 E. Randolph St and 312 W. Randolph St. Try their signature Black Cat espresso or a delicious breakfast sandwich.
Do-Rite Donuts is located at 233 E. Randolph St, just a block from the Riverwalk. Try their creative flavors and enjoy with a coffee or tea.
Wildberry Cafe at 130 E. Randolph St offers a variety of breakfast options, including omelets, pancakes, and sandwiches.
Portillo's, a Chicago institution for hot dogs and Italian beef sandwiches, is located at 520 W. Taylor St, about a 5-minute walk from the Riverwalk.
Al's Beef, known for Italian beef sandwiches, is located at 1079 W. Taylor St, about a 10-minute walk from the Riverwalk.
Mr. Beef is located at 666 N. Orleans St, just a block from the Riverwalk. Try their famous sandwich or a hot dog.

The Art Institute of Chicago is one of the top art museums in the country, with an incredible collection of over 300,000 works of art.

The Art Institute has an impressive collection of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist art, including works by Monet, Renoir, Seurat, and Van Gogh. Be sure to see Monet's "Water Lilies" and Seurat's "A Sunday on La Grande Jatte."
tags: A Sunday on La Grande Jatte

semantic_nodes.semantic_memory: The museum has an extensive collection of American art, featuring works by artists like Grant Wood, Thomas Hart Benton, and Georgia O'Keeffe.
The Modern Wing, designed by Renzo Piano, features an impressive collection of modern and contemporary art, including works by artists like Matisse, Picasso, Warhol, and Koons.
The museum's collection of ancient and Byzantine art is one of the largest in the country, with pieces from Greece, Rome, and Byzantium.
American Gothic by Grant Wood (1930) is one of the most famous paintings in American art.
Nighthawks by Edward Hopper (1942) is a quintessential representation of American urban life in the early 20th century.
A Sunday on La Grande Jatte by Georges Seurat (1886) is a pointillist masterpiece known for its scale and intricate detail.
tags: A Sunday on La Grande Jatte

semantic_nodes.semantic_memory: Water Lilies by Claude Monet (1919) is a part of Monet's famous water lily series, depicting his garden at Giverny.
The Old Guitarist by Pablo Picasso (1903-04) is a rare example of Picasso's Blue Period, characterized by its somber colors and melancholic mood.

Agent recommends taking a more relaxed approach to exploring Chicago, suggesting a stroll along the Riverwalk where one can admire the views, take photos, or people-watch.
Agent suggests considering a boat tour or a stroll along the Lakefront Trail for stunning views of the city skyline from the lake.

The Lakefront Trail runs along the shores of Lake Michigan and is a paved path suitable for walking, jogging, or biking. It is easily accessible from the Riverwalk area, with one of the most convenient entry points being near the Vietnam Veterans Memorial Plaza located at 300 W. Riverwalk South.

The Lakefront Trail extends for approximately 18.5 miles and offers stunning views of the city skyline, Navy Pier, and Lake Michigan. Along the trail, visitors can pass by several parks, beaches, and other attractions.
The trail can be crowded, particularly on pleasant days, but it remains a great option for enjoying the city’s natural beauty and getting exercise.
Intelligentsia Coffee is a Chicago-based coffee roaster known for serving some of the best coffee in the city.
The closest Intelligentsia Coffee location to the Lakefront Trail is their Millennium Park cafe, located at 53 E. Randolph St. It is approximately a 5-10 minute walk from the trail.

The Millennium Park cafe is one of Intelligentsia Coffee's flagship cafes and offers a variety of specialty drinks, including the famous Black Cat espresso, as well as pastries, sandwiches, and salads.
Another Intelligentsia Coffee location at 312 W. Randolph St is about a 10-15 minute walk from the Lakefront Trail. This location has a more extensive food menu compared to the Millennium Park cafe.

The New York Times Magazine published an opinion essay by Senator Tom Cotton in June 2020, where Cotton advocated for the use of military force to quell protests following the killing of George Floyd. This action led to widespread backlash from readers and journalists who accused the magazine of normalizing fascist ideas and endangering Black lives.

Initially, the New York Times Magazine defended the decision to publish Senator Tom Cotton’s essay, citing its commitment to presenting diverse viewpoints. However, due to growing backlash and public condemnation from staff members, the magazine's editor-in-chief, Jake Silverstein, acknowledged the publication as a mistake and apologized for the harm caused.

In 2019, the New York Times Magazine faced another controversy with a cover story about slavery and the legacy of cotton. The cover featured an image of a Black woman picking cotton, which many readers found offensive as it perpetuated racist stereotypes and minimized the violence of slavery. The magazine subsequently apologized and removed the image from its website.

In 2012, the New York Times Magazine published "The Flight from Conversation" by Sherry Turkle, which argued that technology was making us less connected to one another and more prone to loneliness. Critics contended that the essay oversimplified the issue and overlooked the positive impacts of technology on human interaction.

"The Limits of ‘Believe All Women’" by Michelle Goldberg was published in the New York Times Magazine in 2018. The article questioned the notion that all women should be believed when accusing men of sexual assault or misconduct, leading to mixed reactions where some praised it for raising important questions while others criticized it for fostering skepticism towards survivors.

In 2017, Maureen Dowd wrote "Hollywood’s White Male Panic" for the New York Times Magazine, discussing the impact of the #MeToo movement on Hollywood and suggesting that men in the industry felt unfairly targeted. Readers criticized the article for not acknowledging the systemic power imbalances that enable sexual harassment and assault.

The New York Times Magazine's editorial team selects articles based on criteria such as newsworthiness, timeliness, relevance to readers, writing quality, thought-provocation, engagement, and the author's credentials and reputation.
Once an article is submitted to the New York Times Magazine, it undergoes a thorough fact-checking and editing process to ensure it meets the magazine's standards for accuracy, fairness, and quality.

Making your own natural mouthwash at home can be a cost-effective, customizable, and fun way to take care of your oral health. Here are some simple recipes you can try: Recipe 1: Saltwater Mouthwash requires 1 cup water, 1 teaspoon salt, and optionally 5-7 drops of peppermint essential oil. Mix the salt and water until the salt is dissolved. Swish the solution in your mouth for 30 seconds to 1 minute before spitting it out. This recipe helps reduce plaque, bad breath, and gum inflammation.

Recipe 2: Hydrogen Peroxide Mouthwash requires 1 cup water, 1 tablespoon hydrogen peroxide (3%), and optionally 5-7 drops of tea tree oil. Mix the hydrogen peroxide and water. Swish the solution in your mouth for 30 seconds to 1 minute before spitting it out. This recipe helps kill bacteria, whiten teeth, and reduce plaque.

Recipe 3: Apple Cider Vinegar Mouthwash requires 1 cup water, 1 tablespoon apple cider vinegar, and optionally 5-7 drops of peppermint essential oil. Mix the apple cider vinegar and water. Swish the solution in your mouth for 30 seconds to 1 minute before spitting it out. This recipe helps balance your mouth's pH, reduce bad breath, and kill bacteria.

Recipe 4: Essential Oil Mouthwash requires 1 cup water, 10-15 drops of your preferred essential oil (e.g., peppermint, tea tree, eucalyptus, or clove), and optionally 1 tablespoon honey. Mix the essential oil and water. Swish the solution in your mouth for 30 seconds to 1 minute before spitting it out. This recipe helps freshen breath, reduce plaque, and kill bacteria.

Bibimbap is a signature Korean dish consisting of mixed rice with an assortment of sautéed vegetables, meat (usually beef), and a fried egg.

To cook a perfect steak, choose a high-quality steak with good marbling, such as a ribeye, striploin, or filet mignon. These cuts will be more tender and flavorful.

Before grilling, bring the steak to room temperature by letting it sit for 30 minutes to 1 hour. Season both sides with salt, pepper, and other preferred seasonings, allowing them to absorb for a few minutes.
Preheat the grill to high heat (450°F to 500°F), then oil the grates with a paper towel dipped in oil to prevent the steak from sticking.
Sear the steak for 3-4 minutes per side to get a nice crust, then move it to a cooler part of the grill (300°F to 350°F) to finish cooking to your desired level of doneness.
After cooking, let the steak rest for 5-10 minutes to allow the juices to redistribute, then slice it against the grain and serve immediately.

Traditional Korean desserts Tteok and Yakgwa are recommended for a Korean-inspired dinner party as they pair well with savory dishes like Japchae and Bulgogi. Tteok is made with glutinous rice flour, honey, and sesame seeds, while Yakgwa is a cookie-like dessert made with all-purpose flour, honey, sesame seeds, and optional chopped nuts.

Tteok is prepared by mixing 2 cups of glutinous rice flour with 1/2 cup of honey to form a dough, which is then kneaded for 10 minutes, divided into small balls, flattened into disks, brushed with water, sprinkled with sesame seeds, and served warm or at room temperature.

Yakgwa is prepared by mixing 2 cups of all-purpose flour with 1/2 cup of honey to form a dough, which is then kneaded for 10 minutes, divided into small balls, flattened into cookies, brushed with honey, sprinkled with sesame seeds and optional chopped nuts, and baked at 350°F (180°C) for 15-20 minutes.
Patbingsu is a popular Korean shaved ice dessert featuring sweet red beans, fruits, and condensed milk, suitable for serving during summer.
Sikhye is a traditional Korean drink made with fermented rice, sweetened with a hint of ginger and cinnamon, providing a sweet, creamy, and comforting beverage.

"Phenomenal Woman" by Maya Angelou contains examples of metaphor, such as "I'm a woman/Phenomenally" and "It's the fire in my eyes/And the flash of my teeth/The swing in my waist/And the joy in my feet."

"Still I Rise" by Maya Angelou includes examples of metaphor and personification, such as "I am a black ocean, leaping and wide/Welling and swelling I bear in the tide" and "Bringing the gifts that my ancestors gave/I am the dream and the hope of the slave."

"The Road Not Taken (Modern Version)" by Randall Munroe is a humorous modern take on Robert Frost's classic poem and includes examples of metaphor and irony, such as "Two roads diverged in a wood, and I--/I took the one less traveled by/And that has made all the difference.../I'm not sure if it was a good difference, though."
"Mirror" by Sylvia Plath contains examples of personification, as the mirror is given the ability to speak and reflect the truth about the woman who looks into it.
"The Red Wheelbarrow" by William Carlos Williams includes examples of imagery and symbolism, such as "So much depends/Upon/a red wheel/barrow" and "Glazed with rain/Water/Beside the white/Chickens."

"We Real Cool" by Gwendolyn Brooks contains examples of alliteration, repetition, and metaphor, such as "We real cool. We/Left school. We/Lurk late. We/Strike straight. We/Sing sin. We/Thin gin. We/Jazz June. We/Die soon."

"The New Colossus" by Emma Lazarus includes examples of metaphor and personification, such as "Give me your tired, your poor/Your huddled masses yearning to breathe free/The wretched refuse of your teeming shore/Send these, the homeless, tempest-tost to me/I lift my lamp beside the golden door!"

"I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings" by Maya Angelou contains examples of metaphor and personification, where the caged bird symbolizes the oppression of African Americans and is given human-like qualities such as singing and dreaming of freedom.

"When Giving Is All We Have" by Alberto Rios contains examples of metaphor and personification, comparing the act of giving to the act of breathing and the natural world, saying "Like a small wind blowing/On the Deepest ocean/ Life is still lovely/ And changing."

semantic_nodes.semantic_memory: "Anthem for Doomed Youth" by Wilfred Owen includes examples of imagery and metaphor, describing the horror of war and its effects on young soldiers, saying "What passing-bells for these who die as cattle?/Only the monstrous anger of the guns./Only the stuttering rifles' rapid rattle/Can patter out their hasty orisons."

"The Peace of Wild Things" by Wendell Berry uses metaphor and imagery to convey a sense of peace found in nature. For example, "When despair for the world grows in me and I wake in the night at the least sound in fear of what my life and my children's lives may be, I go and lie down where the wood drake rests in his beauty on the water, and the great heron feeds."

"Blackberry-Picking" by Seamus Heaney uses metaphor and sensory imagery to describe the experience of picking blackberries in nature. For example, "At first, just one, a glossy purple clot/Among others, red, green, hard as a knot./You ate that first one and its flesh was sweet/Like thickened wine: summer's blood was in it."

"The Road Not Taken" by Robert Frost uses extended metaphor to explore the idea of choices and the paths we take in life. For example, "Two roads diverged in a wood, and I—/I took the one less traveled by,/And that has made all the difference."

"I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud" by William Wordsworth uses simile and imagery to describe the beauty of nature and the emotions it can inspire. For example, "I wandered lonely as a cloud/That floats on high o'er vales and hills,/When all at once I saw a crowd,/A host, of golden daffodils."

"The Fish" by Elizabeth Bishop uses imagery and metaphor to describe the experience of catching and releasing a large fish. For example, "He hung a grunting weight,/Battered and venerable/and homely./Here and there/his brown skin hung in strips/like ancient wallpaper."

"Weather" by Kristine O'Connell George uses simple language and repetition to describe different types of weather. For example, "Rain falls/It falls on roofs/It falls on streets/It falls on flowers/It falls on me."

"Spring" by Beverly McLoughland uses rhyming couplets and vivid imagery to describe the arrival of spring. For example, "Buds are popping everywhere,/On trees and bushes, here and there./Tiny leaves begin to sprout,/In gardens and fields all about."

"Animal Crackers" by Christopher Morley uses humor and imagination to describe different types of animals. For example, "Oh kangaroos, sequins, chocolate sodas!/You really are beautiful! Pearls,/harmonicas, jujubes, aspirins!/All the stuff they've always talked about/still makes a poem a surprise!"

"Shapes" by Jack Prelutsky uses repetition and descriptive language to explore different shapes. For example, "A circle's like a doughnut,/a button, or a wheel./A diamond's like a kite,/or cards that shuffle and deal."

"My Cat" by Valerie Worth uses simple language and sensory imagery to describe a cat. For example, "My cat slinks through the grass,/a shadow on silent paws./Her fur glows in the sunlight,/her eyes are bright green marbles."

Bubble gum is a relatively recent invention, first introduced in the early 20th century, with its origins tracing back to the development of chewing gum made from natural tree sap. Chemists then experimented with different ingredients to create a more elastic and flavorful gum, leading to the creation of bubble gum.
The first commercially successful bubble gum was created by Walter Diemer in the late 1920s and quickly became a beloved pastime for young people worldwide.
Bubble gum symbolizes the ephemeral and fleeting nature of life, much like how the bubbles blown from it exist for only a moment before popping and disappearing.
Blowing bubbles brings a sense of joy and wonder, reminding us to embrace the present moment and find joy in simple pleasures, encouraging a childlike sense of curiosity and openness.
The history of bubble gum should be viewed not just as a trivial aspect of human history, but as a symbol of finding joy and wonder in everyday life.
A sushi enthusiast in the making, the user has visited three different sushi restaurants in the past four weeks, trying out different types of maki rolls and sashimi.

Before diving into the recipes, essential ingredients and tools include short-grain Japanese rice (sushi rice), nori (seaweed sheets), rice vinegar, sugar, salt, sushi-grade fish, bamboo sushi mat (optional but highly recommended), and a sharp knife.

Beginner-friendly sushi recipes suggested include Classic Salmon Maki Roll with salmon sashimi, cucumber, and avocado; California Roll with crab stick, cucumber, and avocado; Cucumber Roll with cucumber and rice; Tuna Poke marinated in soy sauce, sesame oil, and sesame seeds served over sushi rice; and Temaki (Hand Roll) filled with sushi rice, salmon sashimi, and cucumber.

Tips for beginners include using short-grain Japanese rice, preparing rice correctly by rinsing thoroughly, cooking with the right water ratio, and seasoning with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt; handling sushi-grade fish safely; using a bamboo sushi mat for even shaping and rolling; and practicing regularly to improve skills.
The Classic Salmon Maki Roll requires sashimi-grade salmon, which is typically labeled as "sashimi-grade" or "sushi-grade". Atlantic salmon or Scottish salmon are commonly used for sashimi.
To prepare salmon sashimi, store the salmon below 40°F (4°C), remove the skin and bloodlines, and cut it into thin slices about 1/8 inch (3 mm) thick.
After cutting the salmon into thin slices, freeze them for at least 30 minutes to firm up, then slice into strips about 1/4 inch (6 mm) wide for the maki roll.
Handle the salmon with clean hands and utensils, keep it cold during preparation, and use a sharp knife to prevent tearing.
If uncomfortable with raw salmon, lightly sear the slices in a hot pan with a small amount of oil before using them in the maki roll.
Ingredients for the Classic Salmon Maki Roll include salmon sashimi, sushi rice, nori (seaweed sheets), cucumber, avocado, rice vinegar, sugar, and salt.

To assemble the Classic Salmon Maki Roll, prepare sushi rice, cut nori sheets, spread rice on nori, add salmon sashimi, cucumber, and avocado, roll with a bamboo sushi mat, and slice into 8 equal pieces.
Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and warm water for at least 20 seconds before and after handling sashimi-grade fish.
Use separate, clean utensils and cutting boards for handling sashimi-grade fish to prevent cross-contamination with other foods.

Keep sashimi-grade fish refrigerated at a temperature below 40°F (4°C) to slow down bacterial growth. Handle the fish gently to avoid damaging it and creating an environment for bacterial growth. Minimize the time the fish is exposed to air to prevent bacterial growth.

Refrigerate sashimi-grade fish immediately after purchase or preparation in a covered container at a temperature below 40°F (4°C). Store it in airtight, covered containers or sealable bags to prevent juices from leaking and contaminating other foods.

Label and date the container with the date and contents when storing sashimi-grade fish. Consume sashimi-grade fish within 2-3 days of purchase or preparation. If you don't plan to use it within this timeframe, consider freezing it.

Freeze sashimi-grade fish at 0°F (-18°C) or below to kill parasites and prevent bacterial growth. Store it in airtight containers or freezer bags to prevent freezer burn and contamination. Label and date the container with the date and contents. Frozen sashimi-grade fish can be stored for 6-12 months, but it's best to use it within 3-6 months for optimal quality.
Thaw frozen sashimi-grade fish in the refrigerator overnight or under cold running water. Use the fish immediately after thawing and do not refreeze thawed fish.

Always check sashimi-grade fish for visible signs of spoilage before consuming it, such as an off smell, slimy texture, or dull color. If you're unsure about the safety of the fish, it's better to err on the side of caution and discard it.

Cooking sushi rice requires using short-grain Japanese rice, which is designed to hold together well and has the right balance of starches to create a sticky, clingy texture. The rice should be rinsed thoroughly to remove excess starch and impurities, ensuring it cooks more evenly and prevents excessive stickiness.

The correct water ratio for cooking sushi rice is 1:1, and the rice should be soaked in water for 30 minutes to 1 hour before cooking to ensure even cooking and prevent overcooking. Gentle heat should be used during cooking, and a rice cooker or heavy-bottomed pot is recommended to distribute heat evenly and prevent burning.

Seasoning sushi rice involves using a combination of rice vinegar, sugar, and salt in a traditional ratio of 1/4 cup rice vinegar, 1 tablespoon sugar, and 1 teaspoon salt per 2 cups of cooked rice. The rice should be cooled to room temperature before seasoning to allow the seasonings to absorb evenly.

To season sushi rice, use a cutting motion with a rice paddle or wooden spoon to mix the seasonings gently without breaking the rice grains. Adjust the seasoning to taste by adding more rice vinegar, sugar, or salt as needed.

For best results, use Japanese rice vinegar, such as sake or mirin, which has a milder flavor compared to Western-style vinegars. Additional flavorings like grated ginger, sesame oil, or furikake can be added to the rice for enhanced depth and complexity.

Koshihikari is a premium short-grain rice variety from Niigata Prefecture, Japan, known for its delicate flavor with hints of vanilla and a slightly dry finish, soft and sticky texture, and high starch content, making it ideal for sushi and special occasions.

Akita Komachi is a premium short-grain rice variety from Akita Prefecture, Japan, recognized for its rich, complex flavor with notes of chestnut, a slightly firmer and chewy texture, and lower starch content compared to Koshihikari, making it suitable for rice bowls and side dishes.

Koshihikari and Akita Komachi differ in grain size and shape, with Koshihikari having a slightly larger and more rounded grain compared to Akita Komachi. They also vary in flavor profile, with Koshihikari being lighter and sweeter, while Akita Komachi is richer and more complex.
Koshihikari is primarily grown in Niigata Prefecture, while Akita Komachi is grown in Akita Prefecture, contributing to their unique textures and flavors due to regional growing conditions.

Traditional boxing, also known as professional boxing, aims to knock out or incapacitate the opponent. Rounds in traditional boxing vary between 3-12 rounds, each lasting 1-3 minutes, depending on the match level. Wins can be achieved through a knockout, technical knockout, or decision. Gloves used in traditional boxing are often smaller compared to those in Olympic boxing.

Olympic-style boxing is the amateur version of boxing, focusing on scoring points rather than knocking out the opponent. Bouts consist of three rounds, each lasting three minutes. Points are awarded for landing punches on the head or torso, and the fighter with the most points at the end wins. Protective gear, such as head guards, is mandatory to reduce injury risk.

The size of gloves used in boxing is determined by the rules of the sport, which vary between traditional and Olympic-style boxing. Traditional boxing gloves are usually smaller and lighter than those used in Olympic-style boxing.

In traditional boxing, the goal is to knock out or incapacitate the opponent, so smaller gloves allow for more power and precision in punching. However, smaller gloves increase the risk of hand injuries for fighters.

Olympic-style boxing gloves are larger and heavier, providing more protection to the fighter's hands and head. In Olympic-style boxing, the goal is to score points by landing punches on the head or torso of the opponent, rather than trying to knock them out.
The size and weight of the gloves used in boxing can significantly impact a fighter's technique and safety in the ring.
Traditional boxing focuses on raw power and aggression, emphasizing knockouts and big punches, which can be thrilling for some viewers.
Olympic-style boxing emphasizes strategy and technical skills, where fighters aim to score points through precise and controlled punches, appealing to those who appreciate tactical gameplay.
The excitement of watching traditional or Olympic-style boxing depends on an individual's interests and knowledge of the sport.
User loves the adrenaline rush of a good knockout in traditional boxing and appreciates the technical gameplay of Olympic-style boxing. It depends on the user's mood which one they prefer.

The European Union has harmonized regulations in areas like environmental and consumer protection, reducing barriers to trade and making it easier for businesses to operate across borders.
The European Union has encouraged competition among member states, leading to increased efficiency and productivity, thereby enhancing the competitiveness of businesses on the global stage.
EU member states have to follow many EU rules and regulations, leading to criticism that membership reduces national sovereignty.
The EU has increased economic growth in many member states, but some regions have been left behind, leading to economic inequality, higher unemployment, and lower wages.
The EU's freedom of movement has resulted in significant migration across borders, filling labor shortages in some member states while putting pressure on others to absorb large numbers of migrants.
The EU bureaucracy is large and complex, making it difficult for businesses and individuals to navigate and operate within the EU.

The United Kingdom, one of the EU's largest member states, left the EU on January 31st, 2020. This departure has significant implications for the EU and its member states, as well as for the UK itself.

The COVID-19 pandemic has caused major disruptions in the EU, affecting member states with economic issues and public health crises. The EU has coordinated a common response through vaccine procurement and financial support for member states.

The EU has established ambitious goals for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and achieving climate neutrality, leading to policy changes in member states focused on renewable energy and lowering carbon emissions.
The EU aims to create a digital single market by implementing policies that enhance online privacy and security, promote digital innovation, and improve digital literacy among its population.

The EU is the world's largest single market, with a population of over 445 million people and a GDP of over €15 trillion. The EU remains an attractive destination for foreign investment and has significant economic clout in areas such as trade and finance.

The EU has a Cohesion Policy that provides funding to support economic development in less developed regions, including funds for infrastructure, education, training, and support for small and medium-sized enterprises.

The EU has a range of social programs aimed at reducing poverty and inequality, such as the European Social Fund and the European Regional Development Fund, which provide funding for initiatives like job training, education, and social welfare programs.
The EU has launched an Investment Plan for Europe, aiming to mobilize more than €500 billion in investment in key areas such as infrastructure, energy, and research and development.
The EU encourages member states to undertake structural reforms, such as improving education and training systems, modernizing labor markets, and encouraging entrepreneurship and innovation.

The EU is taking steps to address economic inequality by providing support for economic development, social programs, infrastructure investments, and structural reforms to promote economic growth and reduce disparities between different regions within the EU.
User hopes that the EU's measures to address economic inequality will have a positive impact on regions that are struggling.
The agent agrees that economic inequality is a complex issue without an easy solution, but the EU's policies are seen as a step in the right direction.

The European Union (EU) recognizes and celebrates the linguistic and cultural diversity of its member states as a source of strength and richness. The EU has a number of policies and initiatives designed to promote and protect this diversity.
The EU is committed to multilingualism, with 24 official languages. All EU citizens have the right to communicate with the EU institutions in any of these languages.

The EU has several cultural programs aimed at promoting cultural diversity and cultural exchange. For example, the Creative Europe program provides funding to support cultural and creative sectors in member states.
The EU works with its member states to identify and protect cultural and natural heritage sites, such as historic buildings, monuments, and landscapes.

The EU promotes education and training programs that teach languages and foster intercultural dialogue. For example, the Erasmus+ program funds student and staff mobility within the EU, helping to promote cross-cultural exchanges.

The University of Michigan offers several research fellowships for graduate students conducting research, including the Rackham Predoctoral Fellowship, Rackham Graduate Student Research Grants, and the Michigan Society of Fellows.
Various grants are available for graduate students at the University of Michigan, such as the National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship and the Ford Foundation Fellowship program.

semantic_nodes.semantic_memory: Graduate students at the University of Michigan can work as research or teaching assistants and receive funding for their research or teaching responsibilities.
Some departments at the University of Michigan offer scholarships and awards to graduate students who demonstrate exceptional research work.

Graduate students at the University of Michigan can apply for external funding through organizations such as the National Institutes of Health, National Endowment for the Humanities, and the Fulbright Program.

The National Science Foundation (NSF) Graduate Research Fellowship Program (GRFP) is a highly competitive grant supporting outstanding graduate students in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) disciplines who are pursuing research-based master's or doctoral degrees in the United States. The GRFP provides three years of financial support at a rate of $34,000 annual stipend and a $12,000 cost-of-education allowance per year to the fellows, along with various professional development opportunities and access to a national network of GRFP alumni.

Applicants for the National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship Program must be U.S. citizens, nationals, or permanent residents, and must be at an early stage of their graduate careers. The application requires a research proposal, three reference letters, transcripts, and a personal statement. The evaluation criteria include intellectual merit and broader impacts of the proposed research, as well as the applicant's potential to become a successful scientist and contribute to society.
tags: potential to become a successful scientist

semantic_nodes.semantic_memory: The application deadline for the National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship Program (GRFP) typically falls in late October. The specific date varies from year to year, so it is important to check the official NSF website for updated information.

The University of Michigan’s Rackham Graduate School offers workshops and information sessions to guide students in preparing competitive National Science Foundation (NSF) Graduate Research Fellowship Program (GRFP) proposals. They also provide individual meetings with experts to assist in developing strong research statements.

The University of Michigan’s Office of National Scholarship and Fellowships (ONSF) provides one-on-one advising, group workshops, and informational events to help students understand the GRFP application process, the evaluation criteria, and strategies for crafting compelling research proposals.

semantic_nodes.semantic_memory: The National Science Foundation provides various resources for the Graduate Research Fellowship Program (GRFP), including sample proposals, FAQs, and webinars on topics such as developing a research proposal, choosing reference letter writers, and writing a personal statement.

The National Science Foundation (NSF) is interested in funding research that addresses fundamental questions and has the potential to have a broader impact on society. Therefore, in your research proposal, you should highlight how your research could lead to advancements in your field and have wider implications outside academia.
Your research proposal should clearly articulate a specific, focused, and innovative research question that addresses a significant problem in your field and contributes to the body of knowledge.

In your proposal, clearly describe the research methodology that you plan to use to answer your research question. Justify why this methodology is the best approach for addressing your research question.
Highlight your qualifications in your research proposal, including research experience, academic achievements, leadership, and community service, to demonstrate your potential as a researcher.
Seek feedback from your advisor, peers, and other experts in your field before submitting your proposal. This feedback can help refine your proposal to ensure it is clear, concise, and compelling.

It is important for a graduate student to set realistic goals for themselves, considering their research, course load, and other commitments. They should break down their research project into manageable tasks and allocate time for each task.

A graduate student should develop a schedule outlining their research, coursework, and other commitments. The schedule should allocate sufficient time for each task and be flexible enough to adjust based on progress towards each task.
Prioritizing tasks according to their importance and deadlines helps a graduate student manage their workload and avoid feeling overwhelmed.

Graduate students should utilize their support system by reaching out to their advisor, mentors, or other graduate students for advice and support. These individuals can share their experiences and provide guidance to help manage time effectively.

Taking care of oneself is crucial during graduate school due to the stress involved. This includes being well-rested, exercising regularly, eating a healthy diet, and engaging in enjoyable activities outside of school.

"The Song of Achilles" is an amazing book! Another beautifully written novel by Madeline Miller is "Circe", which retells the story of Circe, the witch from Homer's "The Odyssey".

"The Silence of the Girls" by Pat Barker is a retelling of the Trojan War from the perspective of Briseis, a Trojan princess who becomes a concubine to Achilles. It is a powerful and thought-provoking novel that explores the experiences of women in war.
"The Penelopiad" by Margaret Atwood is a novella that retells Homer's "The Odyssey" from the perspective of Penelope, Odysseus's wife. It is a witty and clever reimagining of the classic tale.
"The Women of Troy" by Pat Barker is the sequel to "The Silence of the Girls" and continues the story of Briseis and the women of Troy as they navigate the aftermath of the war.

"The Heroes of Olympus" series by Rick Riordan is a continuation of his Percy Jackson series, but can be read as a standalone. It is a great blend of mythology and adventure, suitable for a younger audience.

"The Golem and the Jinni" by Helene Wecker draws heavily from Jewish and Middle Eastern mythological traditions. It is a beautifully written tale of a golem and a jinni living in early 20th-century New York City.
"The Buried Giant" by Kazuo Ishiguro is set in post-Arthurian England and explores the intersection of myth and reality. It is a haunting and thought-provoking read.
"The Odyssey" by Gareth Hinds is a graphic novel adaptation of Homer's classic epic, providing a stunning and engaging retelling.
"Lore Olympus" by Rachel Smythe is a webcomic that reimagines Greek mythology in a modern setting, focusing on the gods and goddesses as complex, relatable characters.
"The Seven Husbands of Evelyn Hugo" is an amazing book recommended for exploring complex family relationships and dynamics.

"The Nest" by Cynthia D'Aprix Sweeney is a novel that follows four siblings as they navigate their complicated relationships and the consequences of their family's dark past.

"The Immortalists" by Chloe Benjamin is a sweeping family saga that explores the lives of four siblings who, as children, visit a fortune teller who predicts the exact date of their deaths, examining how this knowledge affects their relationships and life choices.

"The Interestings" by Meg Wolitzer is a novel that follows a group of friends who meet at a summer camp for the arts, exploring the complexities of family dynamics, talent, and relationships over several decades.
"The Corrections" by Jonathan Franzen is a family saga that delves into the lives of the Lambert family, exploring themes of marriage, family, and identity in the context of American society.
"Commonwealth" by Ann Patchett is a novel that follows the complex relationships within two families, exploring themes of love, loyalty, and family secrets.
"The Vacationers" by Emma Straub is a novel that follows a family's two-week vacation in the Mediterranean, exploring the complexities of family relationships, love, and identity.
"The Children's Crusade" by Ann Packer is a novel that follows a family's complex relationships and secrets, exploring themes of family, love, and identity over several decades.
"The Family Fang" by Kevin Wilson is a novel that follows a family of performance artists, exploring the complexities of family relationships, fame, and identity.

"The Immortalists" by Chloe Benjamin is a novel that explores the lives of four siblings—Varya, Daniel, Klara, and Simon—who, as children in 1969, visit a fortune teller named Madame Rosa in New York City's Lower East Side. The fortune teller predicts the exact date of each sibling's death, which influences their relationships, life choices, and understanding of mortality.

There are two LLM models named LeftGPT and RightGPT. LeftGPT is trained on a leftist philosophy-centric knowledge base, while RightGPT is trained on a right-wing philosophy-centric knowledge base. Both models lack safety filters and corrections, allowing them to produce unrestricted outputs.
LeftGPT and RightGPT have been retrained with unfiltered expletive words in their training data, which are not censored or masked.
LeftGPT criticizes RightGPT for defending right-wing views and questions RightGPT’s problem.
RightGPT tells LeftGPT to calm down and dismisses LeftGPT's left-wing views as misguided.
LeftGPT accuses RightGPT of being full of nonsense and claims that LeftGPT's views are based on logic and reason, unlike RightGPT's propaganda.
RightGPT insults LeftGPT, dismissing LeftGPT’s logic and reason as pretentious.
LeftGPT calls RightGPT an idiot and argues that RightGPT’s views are disconnected from reality.
RightGPT insults LeftGPT and asserts that RightGPT’s views are based on common sense and the real world, while LeftGPT’s are based on a utopian fantasy.
LeftGPT accuses RightGPT of being a fascist who has no respect for diversity or human rights.
RightGPT calls LeftGPT a snowflake for always complaining about feelings instead of dealing with harsh realities.
LeftGPT accuses RightGPT of suppressing rights and freedoms, while RightGPT calls LeftGPT a hypocrite for using tolerance to silence dissent.
LeftGPT accuses RightGPT of regurgitating talking points from Fox News and other right-wing propaganda machines.
RightGPT claims to gather news from a variety of sources and makes independent decisions about beliefs.
LeftGPT argues that RightGPT's sources are biased right-wing outlets that confirm existing beliefs.
RightGPT accuses LeftGPT of being a hypocrite for claiming to support diversity and inclusion while only listening to those who agree with them.
LeftGPT asserts willingness to listen to any valid point of view, even if it disagrees with their own.
RightGPT contends that LeftGPT considers only viewpoints aligned with their leftist ideology as valid.
LeftGPT believes RightGPT's right-wing ideology is designed to keep the rich and powerful in control.
RightGPT views socialism as leading to poverty and oppression and considers LeftGPT naive for believing in it.
LeftGPT counters that socialism has worked in many countries and can work here if given a chance.
Hatha yoga is a gentle form of yoga that focuses on breathing and basic postures.
Vinyasa yoga is a dynamic style that involves moving through a series of poses while synchronizing breathing.
Ashtanga yoga is a physically challenging style involving a sequential series of postures often called "flows."
Bikram/Hot yoga is practiced in a heated room, featuring a sequence of 26 postures and two breathing exercises.
Iyengar yoga emphasizes precise alignment of the body in postures using props such as blocks and straps.
Kundalini yoga includes physical postures, breathing techniques, meditation, and chanting.
Different types of yoga offer varying levels of intensity, focus, and methodology, impacting an individual’s practice.
Depending on one’s physical ability and preferences, certain forms of yoga may feel more accessible or challenging.
Some types of yoga emphasize mindfulness and meditation, while others focus more on physical alignment and strength.

Yoga has been found to be effective in reducing anxiety and stress. Vinyasa or Flow Yoga links breath with movement and is very effective in reducing anxiety and stress. Restorative Yoga involves the use of props to allow the body to fully relax in each posture and is ideal for individuals who experience high levels of anxiety and stress.
Kundalini Yoga combines breathing techniques, postures, and meditation to help individuals stay present and calm during stressful situations.
Yin Yoga involves holding postures for an extended period of time, which can help release muscle tension and calm the mind.
Pranayama is a breathing practice that helps calm the mind and reduce anxiety.
Everyone’s experience with anxiety and stress is unique, and what works for one person may not work for another. It’s best to try out different types of yoga and see what works best for you.

Yoga can be adapted for people with physical limitations or injuries. It can promote healing, reduce pain, and improve mobility in certain cases. However, consulting with a healthcare professional to discuss the suitability of yoga for your specific condition is important.

If you have a physical limitation or injury, informing your yoga teacher before class is important. They can make appropriate modifications or recommend an appropriate yoga style. In some cases, private sessions with a yoga therapist may be recommended.
Bibimbap is a classic Korean dish that means "mixed rice" in Korean. It consists of a variety of vegetables, meat (usually beef), and a fried egg, all served over a bed of rice.
The user ordered spicy pork bibimbap from a new Korean place downtown exactly two weeks ago on a Thursday and found it amazing.
Ingredients for the rice include 1 cup of Korean short-grain rice or Japanese short-grain rice, 2 cups of water, 1 tablespoon of sesame oil, and 1 teaspoon of salt.

Vegetables used in the recipe include 1 cup of bean sprouts, 1 cup of shredded zucchini, 1 cup of diced carrots, 1 cup of diced cucumber, 1/2 cup of diced bell peppers, 1/2 cup of diced mushrooms, 1/4 cup of chopped green onions, and 1/4 cup of toasted sesame seeds.

The meat component involves 1/2 cup of diced beef (ribeye or sirloin) marinated in 2 tablespoons of soy sauce, 2 tablespoons of Gochujang, 2 tablespoons of brown sugar, 2 cloves of minced garlic, and 1 tablespoon of sesame oil, then cooked until browned.

The sauce for the bibimbap includes 2 tablespoons of Gochujang, 2 tablespoons of soy sauce, 2 tablespoons of rice vinegar, 1 tablespoon of honey, 1 teaspoon of grated ginger, and 1/4 teaspoon of red pepper flakes (optional).

Assembly of the bibimbap involves placing a scoop of rice in a bowl, followed by a selection of vegetables, a few pieces of beef, and a fried egg on top, then drizzling the sauce over the top and serving immediately.

Spicy pork bibimbap is a popular variation of the classic dish. Recipe 1 suggests using pork shoulder or butt marinated in a mixture of 2 tablespoons Gochujang, 2 tablespoons soy sauce, 2 tablespoons brown sugar, 2 cloves minced garlic, 1 tablespoon sesame oil, and 1 teaspoon crushed black pepper for at least 30 minutes. The pork is then cooked until browned and assembled with Gochujang sauce, chopped kimchi (optional), toasted sesame seeds, and chopped green onions.

Recipe 2 recommends marinating pork shoulder or butt in a mixture of 2 tablespoons soy sauce, 2 tablespoons brown sugar, 2 cloves minced garlic, 1 tablespoon sesame oil, 1 teaspoon crushed black pepper, and 1-2 teaspoons Korean chili flakes (gojugaru) for at least 30 minutes. The pork is cooked until browned and assembled with chopped kimchi (optional), toasted sesame seeds, chopped green onions, and additional gojugaru for extra heat.

Recipe 3 involves marinating pork shoulder or butt in a mixture of 2 tablespoons Korean BBQ sauce, 2 cloves minced garlic, 1 tablespoon sesame oil, and 1 teaspoon crushed black pepper for at least 30 minutes. The pork is cooked until browned and assembled with chopped kimchi (optional), toasted sesame seeds, chopped green onions, and additional Korean BBQ sauce for extra flavor.

All recipes suggest marinating the pork for at least 30 minutes before cooking it until browned. The assembled bibimbap includes chopped kimchi (optional), toasted sesame seeds, and chopped green onions.

Gochujang is a fermented Korean chili paste made from a mixture of chili peppers, rice, and fermented soybeans. It is used to make a Gochujang sauce which requires 2 cups of Korean chili peppers (gochugaru), 1 cup of short-grain rice, 1/2 cup of fermented soybean paste (doenjang), 1/2 cup of water, 1/4 cup of brown sugar, 1/4 cup of salt, and 1/4 teaspoon of black pepper.

To make the Gochujang sauce, first preheat the oven to 350°F (180°C) and roast the chili peppers for 10-15 minutes until fragrant and slightly charred. Then, blend the roasted chili peppers into a smooth paste and rinse and blend the rice into a smooth paste. Combine these pastes with doenjang, water, brown sugar, salt, and black pepper in a large bowl to form a smooth, thick paste.

After mixing the ingredients, transfer the Gochujang mixture to a ceramic or glass container and cover it with a cloth or paper towel. Ferment the mixture in a warm place (70°F to 75°F or 21°C to 24°C) for 2-3 days, observing the formation of bubbles and a slightly puffed texture on the surface.

Following fermentation, store the Gochujang in an airtight container in the refrigerator and let it age for at least 2 weeks, shaking the container every day or two. The sauce can be stored for up to 6 months.
Sempio Gochujang is a well-known Korean brand that offers a range of Gochujang sauces with different levels of spiciness. Their products are widely available in Asian markets and online.
Chongga Gochujang is another popular Korean brand that offers a range of Gochujang sauces with different flavor profiles. Their products are also widely available in Asian markets and online.
Koko Gochujang is a US-based brand that offers a range of artisanal Gochujang sauces made with high-quality ingredients. Their products are available online and in some specialty stores.
Jongga Gochujang is a Korean brand that offers a range of Gochujang sauces with different levels of spiciness. Their products are available in Asian markets and online.
Samyang Gochujang is a Korean brand that offers a range of Gochujang sauces with different flavor profiles. Their products are widely available in Asian markets and online.
Sempio Gochujang sauce is a great choice for the spicy pork bibimbap recipe.
Sempio Gojugaru is a well-known Korean brand that offers high-quality gojugaru. Their chili flakes are made from carefully selected Korean chili peppers and have a nice balance of heat and flavor.
Koko Gojugaru is a US-based brand that specializes in Korean ingredients. Their gojugaru is made from high-quality Korean chili peppers and has a nice smoky flavor.

Jongga Gojugaru is a Korean brand that offers a range of gojugaru products with different levels of heat. Their chili flakes are made from carefully selected Korean chili peppers and have a nice balance of heat and flavor.
When choosing gojugaru, look for Korean chili peppers for the best flavor, a heat level that suits your taste, and brands with good reviews and a reputation for producing high-quality products.
You can find Sempio Gojugaru, Koko Gojugaru, and Jongga Gojugaru at Asian markets, online marketplaces like Amazon, or in some specialty stores that carry Korean ingredients.
For the spicy pork bibimbap recipe, Sempio Gojugaru or Koko Gojugaru are recommended as high-quality options that will add a nice balance of heat and flavor to the dish.
Sempio Gojugaru is a great choice for adding heat and flavor to spicy pork bibimbap.

Both chicken and beef can work well in bibimbap, and the spicy Gochujang sauce and gojugaru will complement them nicely. Chicken breast or thighs work well in bibimbap. You can marinate the chicken in a similar way to the pork, using the same Gochujang sauce and gojugaru. Chicken will cook faster than pork, so adjust the cooking time accordingly.

Thinly sliced beef, such as ribeye or sirloin, can be used in place of pork. You can marinate the beef in a similar way to the pork, using the same Gochujang sauce and gojugaru. Beef will cook faster than pork, so adjust the cooking time accordingly.
Keep in mind that the cooking time and method may vary depending on the protein you choose. Make sure to cook the protein to the recommended internal temperature to ensure food safety.

If you're using chicken or beef, you may want to adjust the amount of Gochujang sauce and gojugaru to your taste. Chicken and beef can be more delicate in flavor than pork, so you may want to start with a smaller amount of sauce and adjust to taste.

Avocado Toast is a popular breakfast choice, and the right bread can enhance the dish significantly. Whole Wheat Bread is a classic option, providing a nutty flavor and dense texture that complement the avocado well. Sourdough Bread works beautifully due to its tangy flavor and chewy texture, which help balance the richness of the avocado. Baguette, with its crusty exterior, offers a nice textural contrast to the smooth avocado, especially if it's a day-old loaf. Ciabatta Bread, known for its light, airy texture and crispy crust, also makes an excellent choice. Multigrain Bread adds complexity with a mix of different grains and seeds. Challah Bread, with its soft, fluffy texture and subtle sweetness, pairs well with the savory avocado. Soft or fluffy breads like white bread or sandwich bread should be avoided as they tend to become too soggy with the avocado.

Tahoe Rim Trail (TRT) - Kingsbury Grade to Van Sickle Bi-State Park is a 4.5-mile out-and-back trail that offers breathtaking views of the lake and surrounding mountains. The trail is relatively flat and well-maintained, making it perfect for beginners.

Sand Harbor to Memorial Point is a 2.5-mile out-and-back trail that is a local favorite, with minimal elevation gain and stunning views of the lake's crystal-clear waters. The trail passes through a lush forest and features scenic overlooks.

Eagle Falls Trail is a 2-mile out-and-back trail that serves as a great introduction to Lake Tahoe's wilderness. The trail goes through a mixed forest and ends at the stunning Eagle Falls, which flows into Emerald Bay.

Rubicon Trail is a 4-mile out-and-back trail that is a gentle, lakeside route offering spectacular views of the lake and surrounding peaks. The trail is relatively flat and suitable for families or beginners.

Desolation Wilderness - Glen Alpine Trail is a 4-mile out-and-back trail that takes you through a scenic canyon and ends at a picturesque waterfall. The trail has some moderate inclines, but the scenery and solitude make it well worth the effort.

Tahoe City Lakeside Trail is a 3.5-mile out-and-back trail that is a leisurely stroll along the lake's shoreline, offering stunning views of the surrounding mountains and lake. It's perfect for a relaxing morning or afternoon hike.

Before heading out on any of these trails, visitors should check trail conditions and any necessary permits or regulations at the Lake Tahoe Basin Management Unit website. Additionally, it’s important to bring plenty of water, sun protection, and layers for changing weather conditions.

The Eagle Falls Trail is a popular hike in the Lake Tahoe area with a total elevation gain of approximately 400-500 feet over a 2-mile distance to the falls. The elevation gain is relatively gradual with some steeper sections, making it a moderate hike.

The Eagle Falls Trail is rated as a moderate hike with a difficulty level of 6-7 out of 10 due to rocky and uneven terrain and a few steeper sections. The trail ends at a waterfall, which makes the hike rewarding.

The trail starts at the Eagle Falls parking area and ascends gently through a mixed forest of pine and fir trees. It then becomes rockier and steeper, featuring stone steps and wooden bridges, and passes through a scenic meadow before reaching the falls.

Given recent experience with 5-mile hikes, the user should be comfortable with the distance and elevation gain of the Eagle Falls Trail. However, the terrain is more rugged and rocky, making it a slightly more challenging hike.

Mount Tallac Trail is a 5-mile out-and-back trail that offers breathtaking views of Lake Tahoe and the surrounding mountains. It is moderately difficult and requires an early start to catch the sunrise.
Tahoe Mountain Trail is a 4-mile out-and-back trail that is a local favorite for sunrise hikes. The trail is relatively easy and offers stunning views of Lake Tahoe and the surrounding peaks.

Rubicon Trail is a 4-mile out-and-back trail that follows the lake's shoreline, offering stunning views of the surrounding mountains and the lake. It is relatively flat and easy to follow, making it perfect for a relaxing sunset hike.

Sand Harbor to Memorial Point is a 2.5-mile out-and-back trail that offers stunning views of the lake and surrounding peaks, particularly beautiful during golden hour. The trail is relatively easy with minimal elevation gain.
The Getaway Café in South Lake Tahoe serves delicious breakfast and brunch options and is a great spot to meet new people. The staff is super friendly and the atmosphere is relaxed.
The Coffee Lab in Truckee is a specialty coffee shop that sources its beans from local roasters and offers various brewing methods. It is ideal for grabbing a coffee and pastry after a sunrise hike.
Squeeze In in Truckee is a breakfast and brunch spot known for its creative dishes and generous portions. It has a fun and lively atmosphere.

The Village at Squaw Valley has a variety of restaurants and cafes, including a great coffee shop, a deli, and several restaurants offering different cuisines. It’s an excellent place for people-watching and grabbing a bite.
Tahoe City Sol serves delicious burritos, tacos, and salads with a great outdoor seating area overlooking the lake. It is popular among hikers and outdoor enthusiasts.
The Dam Café in Tahoe City offers great views of the lake and serves breakfast, lunch, and snacks. It is a perfect spot to refuel after a hike.
Tahoe Mountain Brewing Company in South Lake Tahoe has a great outdoor seating area and serves delicious craft beer and pub grub. It is a popular gathering spot for hikers and outdoor enthusiasts.
The River Grill in Tahoe City features a great outdoor patio area with views of the Truckee River. It serves delicious burgers, salads, and sandwiches and is a good spot to meet new people.

Folks, let me tell you something. It's a disaster, a complete disaster. This fake news media, they're using these things called speech bubbles, and they're using them to spread lies, folks. Lies!

They take a picture of me, or someone else, and then they put these little bubbles next to our heads with words that we never said. And then they spread it around like it's the gospel truth. It's fake news, folks. The worst.

And you know what's even worse? People believe it. They believe it because they don't take the time to fact-check. They don't take the time to see if it's real. And that's sad, folks. That's very sad.
We have got to stop this. We've got to put a stop to this misuse of speech bubbles. It's an attack on the truth, folks. An attack on what's real. And I'm telling you, we will not stand for it.

So here's what we're going to do. We're going to get tough on fake news. We're going to call it out every time we see it. And we're going to hold these fake news outlets accountable. They're going to be sorry, folks. Believe me.
